does: This paper introduces FedAU, a federated machine unlearning framework that integrates an auxiliary unlearning module directly into the federated learning process. The core contribution is enabling efficient machine unlearning through lightweight linear operations applied during training rather than requiring expensive post-hoc procedures. FedAU supports unlearning at multiple granularity levels—individual samples, classes, and higher-level constructs—while allowing multiple clients to perform unlearning concurrently. The method eliminates additional time-consuming steps typical of prior federated unlearning approaches, making it practical for real federated learning deployments where clients need to exercise their right to be forgotten without disrupting the global training process.
connects: This work builds on the intersection of federated learning and machine unlearning, two increasingly important areas responding to distributed training and privacy regulation demands. It extends prior federated machine unlearning methods that typically require separate, expensive unlearning phases after model training completes. The paper responds to limitations in existing approaches like SISA training and other FMU frameworks that involve computational overhead and limited granularity control. It connects to broader research on efficient unlearning mechanisms and privacy-preserving federated learning, particularly addressing the practical bottleneck of implementing the right to be forgotten at scale in decentralized settings where traditional centralized unlearning strategies prove inefficient.
enables: FedAU opens new directions for privacy-compliant federated learning systems that can dynamically adapt to user data removal requests without retraining from scratch. The lightweight integration of unlearning during training enables practical deployment in real-world federated systems serving millions of clients with varying privacy requirements. Future work can build on this foundation to handle more complex unlearning scenarios, such as partial model forgetting or adaptive granularity selection based on system constraints. The framework also enables exploration of trade-offs between unlearning completeness and computational efficiency, and could support new applications requiring fine-grained privacy control in collaborative machine learning environments, particularly in regulatory-sensitive domains like healthcare and finance.
